msDesc.head: Passio s. Eustachii; Haimo on Apocalypse

msItem.title: Life of St. Eustace
msItem.rubric: Incipit passio sanctorum Eustachii cognomento Placidi, uxoris et duorum filiorum
msItem.explicit: (fol. 8r) cui est gloria et potestas, in secula seculorum Amen.
msItem.note: B.H.L. 2760. The text is not identical with Mombritius, Sanctuarium, fol. cclxiiv–cclxvi or Acta Sanctorum Sept VI, pp. 123–35, but has elements of both.
A leaf is missing after fol. 7 (= Acta SS. cit., pp. 134 1. 16–135 1–37). Most of fol. 8r is left blank.

msItem.author: Haimo of Auxerre
msItem.title: Commentary on Apocalypse
msItem.rubric: Incipit expositio domini Haimonis in apokalipsin. Beati Iohannis apostoli et evangeliste, novique prophete

msItem.incipit: Apokalipsis Ihesu Christi. Planior esset sensus si dixisset
Et angelo ecclesie Ephesi… Ephesus interpretatur
Sequitur. Et angelo Laodicie ecclesie scribe. Laodicia interpretatur
Et vidi alium angelum … Angelus Christus est nuncius
Et vidi celum … Celum ut sepissime iam dictum est
msItem.finalRubric: Explicit expositio domini Heimonis in apokalipsin Iohannis. Liber sextus.

msItem.note: Stegmüller Rep. Bibi., no. 3122. PL 117 col. 937–1220. The work is generally attributed now to Haimo of Auxerre.

There are a great many contemporary corrections and insertions over erasures. The scribe left a gap of a few lines on fol. 109. The missing part of the text (PL 117 col. 1102C14–1104A14) has been supplied, mostly on an inserted leaf, fol. 108.

supportDesc.collation: 1⁸ (1 used as pastedown), 2⁸ (1 missing), 3⁸–13⁸, 14⁸ (+1 (fol. 108r) inserted after 5), 15⁸–21⁸, 22¹⁰, quires numbered i-xxii at the end, sometimes in red, or with decorative frames; -dis et simulachris

layoutDesc.layout: 160 105 : 177 leaves: 29 long lines ruled in pencil

handDesc.handNote: Written in Germany in the late 12th cent.

decoDesc.decoNote: Headings in red: lemmata of the text of the Apocalypse underlined in red, except on fol. 177, where they are written in red.

Vinescroll type initial in red and violet ink on fol. 9r, and small initial in blue and red on fol. 113v; decorated red initials on fols. 1r, 8v, 41r, 80v, 98v, 127v, 145v. (Pächt and Alexander i. 93)

bindingDesc.summary:
German, late 12th century inboard binding, date and place of binding as manuscript, with a ‘Romanesque’ structure (cf. Szirmai, pp. 140-142); the sewing supports laced through oblong tunnels in the spine edge of the oak boards. Covered with alum-tawed skin with tabs at head and tail. This is the first binding of the manuscript. Unusually for this type of binding the boards and bookblock do not have canted spine-edge corners for the endbands.
252 175 83 (including tabs).
The bookbinding is described in the order of binding using the controlled vocabulary of the Language of Bindings Thesaurus (LoB).
Bookbinding description by Andrew Honey (Dec. 2023).

binding.endleaves: Left: Integral endleaf, an unfoliated parchment leaf as a pastedown before fol. 1, conjoint with fol. 7.

Right: Separate text-hook endleaf, an unfoliated parchment leaf as a pastedown after fol. 177, its conjoint stub between fols. 167v-168r. The pastedown though blank is pricked and ruled in pencil with 29 lines.
binding.sewing: Sewn all-along with undyed plain thread at five sewing stations on three raised split-strap sewing supports of alum-tawed skin (11 mm wide). Sewing stations (measured in mm from the head-edge at fols. 74v-75r): 21 (kettle), 46 (support 1), 112 (support 2), 175 (support 3), 198 (kettle), 225 (tail). There are no earlier sewing stations in the manuscript and this is the first sewing of the manuscript. A parchment(?) two-hole quire tacket remains in quire 1, visible at fols. 3v-4r.
binding.boards: Wooden oak boards the size of the bookblock, 11-12 mm thick (including covering material), wood identified from grain pattern exposed in recess to right board. Square edge profile to boards. The three sewing supports are laced through oblong tunnels in the spine edge of the boards into channels and holes in a long lacing path (Szirmai fig. 8.10g, p. 153).
binding.edges: Bookblock edges cut flush and undecorated. The boards and bookblock do not have canted spine-edge corners.
binding.spine: The binding has a flat spine shape. Transverse spine linings of thick alum-tawed skin to the outer spine panels at head and tail, with the extensions stuck to the outer faces of the boards. These spine linings act as partial tab stiffeners though there is no evidence that they ever extended above the endbands. Panel spine linings of thick alum-tawed skin to the panels between the sewing supports.
binding.endbands: Sewn endbands worked through the transverse spine lining and sewn on a split-strap core of alum-tawed skin with plain undyed thread (wound-packed-on-two-cores). The endband cores are laced into the boards with a short lacing path (Szirmai fig. 8.12a, p. 155).
binding.covering: Full cover of alum-tawed skin used hairside out (from follicle pattern possibly sheep), now a warm cream colour. The covering is not moulded over the spine and is turned in on all edges with butt or open mitre corners. The covering was not turned in at head and tail of the spine and has been trimmed as a rectangular shape to form a tabbed cap. The tab is lined with brown tanned skin and the perimeter of the two layers is enclosed with blue and undyed plain thread worked in a chevron pattern. There are losses to both the tanned tab lining and perimeter sewing.
binding.fastenings: The binding was held closed with two right-to-left fore-edge clasps, though the upper pin clasp and the end its clasp strap is now lost. Each fastening consisted of a thick short clasp strap of alum-tawed skin (13 mm wide, 2 mm thick) attached to the right board under a slit in the covering with three copper alloy nails through a triangular copper alloy strap plate. A copper alloy pin clasp (decorated and possibly cast) is riveted to the end of the strap with two copper alloy rivets and a rivet plate, and closes on a simple edge pin attached to the left board.
binding.alterations: Quire 1 has been rehitched with thread, and there are leather patches to the fore-edge corners of the right board and the linings of both tabs, the lower clasp strap has been moved and reattached, all presumably modern.
binding.bookmarks: There are three holes in the head tab, probably from now lost thread bookmarks.
binding.chaining: At the head of the right board there is a rebate, stain and scar from a removed iron chain staple or plate.
binding.labels: At the head of the left board two 15th(?)-century title inscriptions in ink, now faded (one and two line). At head of spine a later five-line title inscription in ink. Two printed paper labels to spine ‘31’ (17 x 17 mm) and ‘416’ (9 x 13 mm).

history.provenance: The top of fol. 1, which may have had an inscription, has been cut off.

Formerly in the library of the Premonstratensian monastery of Steinfeld in the Eifel, in the diocese of Cologne, deleted contemporary note on fol. 8r: liber ecclesie sancte Marie sanctique Potentini in Steinvelt. On the front cover is written: 56; on the spine N 9 and on fol. 1: Loc: 255: N: 13 (18th cent.). For the Steinfeld MSS. see P. Bloch, Aachener Kunstblätter 22 (1961), 37–60.
Later acquired by Leander van Ess of Darmstadt: no. 31 in his sale catalogue of 1823.
Bought in 1824 by Sir Thomas Phillipps: no. 416 in his library; and lot 29 in the Phillipps sale, Sotheby’s 6–9 June 1910.
Bought by Lyell in July 1942 from Quaritch; see B. Quaritch, Cat. of MSS., 1931, no. 29 and plate (fol. 9r).
history.acquisition: Chosen as one of the hundred manuscripts bequeathed to the Bodleian by Lyell in 1948.

recordHist.source: Description adapted from A. de la Mare, Catalogue of the Medieval Manuscripts Bequeathed to the Bodleian Library Oxford by James P. R. Lyell (1971); with additions by Andrew Dunning. Catalogue, p. 201 Catalogue, p. 202

ref.title: Manuscripts from German-Speaking Lands – A Polonsky Foundation Digitization Project

revisionDesc.change: Andrew Dunning Revised from description by Albinia de la Mare.

Mitch Fraas Provenance and acquisition information added using https://github.com/littlegustv/oxfordupdates/blob/master/test_case_for_oxford_prov.rb in collaboration with the Mapping Manuscript Migrations project.
James Cummings Up-converted the markup using https://github.com/jamescummings/Bodleian-msDesc-ODD/blob/master/convertTolkien2Bodley.xsl
